title: Chapter 4 Quiz
description: Test your ability to control the flow of C programs!

questions.question: What does an `if` statement do in C?
options: It checks a condition and runs a block of code ONLY if the condition is true.
It creates a new variable called 'if'.
questions.explanation: The `if` statement evaluates the condition inside the `()` and executes the `{}` block if the condition is non-zero (True).

questions.question: What happens if you accidentally type `if (x = 0)` instead of `if (x == 0)`?
options: The code crashes instantly with a Syntax Error.
C automatically corrects the `=` to `==` for you.
The code assigns 0 to `x`, evaluates to False (0), and completely skips the if block.
questions.explanation: A single `=` performs an assignment inline. Since it assigns `x` a value of 0, the final statement evaluation is 0 (False), so it bypasses the block.

questions.question: An `else` block must always be attached directly to the closing brace `}` of an `if` block.
questions.explanation: Yes, `else` cannot exist on its own. It acts as the definitive fallback directly connected to an `if` statement.

questions.question: If you have an `if / else if / else` chain, how many of the code blocks will execute?
options: Only the first one that evaluates to True (or the else).
questions.explanation: Chains are mutually exclusive. The moment C finds the first True condition, it runs that block and purely ignores the rest of the chain.

questions.question: Why might you use a `switch` statement instead of a long `if / else if` chain?
options: The `switch` statement is required for math.
The `switch` statement looks cleaner for handling many exact matches against one variable.
You can check `<` or `>` conditions in a `switch`.
questions.explanation: When comparing one single variable against multiple exact values (like Menu Item 1, 2, 3), a `switch` is much cleaner than chaining 10 `else if`s.

questions.question: You can use relational operators like `>` or `<` inside `case` labels of a `switch` statement.
questions.explanation: Switch cases can ONLY test for strict equality (e.g., `case 1:`, `case 'A':`). You cannot use ranges or logic operators in a case label in standard C.

questions.question: If you forget to write a _____ at the end of a `case` block, the `switch` will 'fall through' to the next case.
questions.explanation: `break;` tells C to violently exit the switch block before hitting the next case.

questions.question: What is the equivalent of the `else` block inside a `switch` statement?
questions.explanation: The `default:` block catches anything that didn't match the preceding cases.

questions.question: What is the correct syntax for the Ternary Operator?
questions.explanation: It reads like a question: "Is the Condition true? (?) Return TrueValue, otherwise (:) Return FalseValue."

questions.question: What does this ternary expression evaluate to: `(10 > 50) ? 100 : 200`
questions.explanation: The condition 10 > 50 is False. Therefore, it selects the right side of the colon, returning 200.

questions.question: If an `if` block's code is only one line long, what are you technically allowed to omit in C?
questions.explanation: C allows you to drop the `{}` for single-line statements, but it is heavily discouraged due to bug risks.

questions.question: Which of these lines uses the Ternary operator perfectly to assign value to `status`?
questions.explanation: The expression evaluates to either 1 or 0, which is perfectly assigned to the integer `status`.

questions.question: It is a good best practice to nest ternary operators deeply within each other.
questions.explanation: False! Nesting ternaries makes them extremely difficult for humans to read. Use if/else for complex logic.

questions.question: In a `switch` block comparing an integer variable `x`, what must follow the word `case`?
options: A constant integer value or character like `1` or `'A'`
questions.explanation: Standard C switch statements require constant values that can be evaluated at compile-time.

questions.question: If you write multiple `case` statements back-to-back without a `break` in between, what does C do?
options: It throws a compile-time syntax error.
It groups them together so that ANY of those values triggers the code below.
questions.explanation: This is called intentional fall-through. If any of those grouped cases matches, it runs the shared block below.

questions.question: To execute code when a tested condition is strictly 0 (False), you can prefix the condition with a Logical NOT operator, written as the _____ symbol.
questions.explanation: The Logical NOT `!` flips False to True and True to False.

questions.question: What happens if both the `if` and its associated `else` block have conditions that are technically true?
options: This is impossible; an `else` block does not have its own condition.
questions.explanation: An `else` block does not take a condition like `else (x == 5)`. It is purely the absolute fallback option. Only `else if` takes a condition.

questions.question: In C, `if (-5)` would evaluate to True.
questions.explanation: In C, ONLY `0` evaluates to False. Any non-zero integer (including negative numbers) is treated as True.

questions.question: Which structure should you use when choosing between 10 completely different specific integers?
questions.explanation: A `switch` statement is structurally perfect and optimized for choosing between many exact primitive options.

questions.question: What does a `break;` statement essentially tell the C compiler to do when inside a `switch` body?
options: Exit the entire program and return to the OS.
Jump completely out of the nearest enclosing `{}` associated with the switch.
questions.explanation: `break` literally 'breaks' the code execution out of the `{}` block, skipping all lines below it until it's outside the scope.
